title: Graph Basics in Java
summary: Adjacency list vs matrix, directed/undirected/weighted, BFS (shortest hops), DFS (explore fully), and cycle detection.

questions.prompt: Adjacency list space complexity for a graph with V vertices and E edges:
questions.explanation: Each vertex stores its neighbour list. Total entries across all lists = E (directed) or 2E (undirected).

questions.prompt: Adjacency matrix space complexity:
questions.explanation: Stores a V×V grid — one cell per possible edge regardless of whether it exists.

questions.prompt: When is an adjacency matrix preferred over an adjacency list?
choices: When the graph is dense (E ≈ V²) or O(1) edge existence check is critical
When the graph is a tree
questions.explanation: Dense graphs fill most of the matrix anyway. O(1) hasEdge(u,v) vs O(degree) for list.

questions.prompt: BFS guarantees shortest path in:
choices: Unweighted graphs — path with fewest hops
questions.explanation: BFS processes nodes level-by-level; level k = all nodes exactly k hops away. First time reached = shortest hop count.

questions.prompt: BFS from node 0 in graph: 0-1, 0-2, 1-3. Visit order:
questions.explanation: Queue: [0] → process 0 → enqueue 1,2 → [1,2] → process 1 → enqueue 3 → [2,3] → process 2 → [3] → process 3. Order: 0,1,2,3.

questions.prompt: DFS explores a graph by:
choices: Processing all neighbours before going deeper
Going as deep as possible along one path before backtracking
questions.explanation: DFS dives to the bottom of one branch, then backtracks to explore other branches — like navigating a maze.

questions.prompt: Mark a node visited in BFS:
choices: When enqueuing it — BEFORE it enters the queue
At the end of the loop
questions.explanation: Marking when enqueuing prevents the same node from being added to the queue multiple times.

questions.prompt: DFS time complexity on a graph with V vertices and E edges:
questions.explanation: Every vertex is visited once (O(V)) and every edge is examined once (O(E)).

questions.prompt: To count connected components in an undirected graph:
choices: Run BFS/DFS from every unvisited node; count how many times you start a new traversal
Check if any node has degree 0
questions.explanation: Each fresh traversal start discovers one new component. Component count = number of starts.

questions.prompt: In undirected cycle detection via DFS, a cycle is found when:
choices: A node is visited twice total
You reach a visited node that is not the direct parent of the current node
questions.explanation: The parent edge looks like a back-edge — exclude it. Any OTHER visited neighbour means a cycle.

questions.prompt: In directed graph cycle detection, 3-colour DFS uses GRAY to mean:
choices: Currently on the active DFS path
questions.explanation: GRAY = in the current recursion stack. Meeting a GRAY node means we found a back-edge = cycle.

questions.prompt: A directed graph with no cycles is called:
questions.explanation: DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph. Topological sort is only possible on DAGs.

questions.prompt: Bipartite graph check can be done with:
choices: BFS 2-colouring — try to colour graph with 2 colours so no adjacent nodes share a colour
questions.explanation: BFS/DFS assigns alternating colours; if any edge connects same-colour nodes, graph is not bipartite.

questions.prompt: DFS from 0 in: 0→1, 0→2, 1→3. Visit order (recursive, process neighbours left-to-right):
questions.explanation: DFS: visit 0 → go to 1 → go to 3 (dead end, backtrack) → backtrack to 0 → go to 2. Order: 0,1,3,2.

questions.prompt: An undirected graph is connected if:
choices: Every node has at least one edge
BFS/DFS from any single node visits ALL vertices
questions.explanation: Connected = one component. Single BFS/DFS should colour every vertex.

questions.prompt: Iterative DFS uses an explicit stack instead of recursion to:
choices: Avoid StackOverflowError on very deep/large graphs
questions.explanation: Recursive DFS can overflow the JVM call stack on graphs with millions of nodes. Explicit stack uses heap memory.

questions.prompt: In a directed graph, edge u→v means:
choices: v is also connected back to u
You can travel from u to v but NOT necessarily from v to u
u and v are in the same component
u has higher priority than v
questions.explanation: Directed = one-way street. Reachability is asymmetric.

questions.prompt: Which traversals can detect cycles in a graph?
choices: Topological sort — cycle exists if not all nodes processed
questions.explanation: BFS with just visited set detects connectivity but needs parent-tracking to detect undirected cycles reliably.

questions.prompt: Flood fill (paint bucket tool) is which graph algorithm?
choices: BFS or DFS on a grid graph
questions.explanation: Grid cells are nodes; adjacent cells sharing the same colour are edges. BFS/DFS fills the connected region.

questions.prompt: BFS vs DFS — which uses more memory for a wide, shallow graph?
choices: BFS — its queue can hold an entire level which may be very wide
questions.explanation: BFS queue grows to the width of the current frontier. DFS stack depth equals the longest path — short for shallow graphs.

flashcards.back: List<List<Integer>>. Space O(V+E). Best for sparse graphs (most real graphs).
int[V][V]. Space O(V²). O(1) edge lookup. Best for dense graphs.
Queue + visited set. Level k = all nodes exactly k hops away. Shortest path (unweighted).
Stack/recursion + visited set. Goes deep first, backtracks at dead ends.
Mark node visited when adding to BFS queue — prevents duplicate entries.
Count how many fresh BFS/DFS starts are needed to visit all nodes.
DFS: back-edge to a visited non-parent node = cycle.
WHITE=unvisited, GRAY=in stack, BLACK=done. GRAY neighbour = cycle.

project.brief:
Write GraphsLab.java using adjacency list (List<List<Integer>>):
1. buildGraph(int n, int[][] edges, boolean directed) → List<List<Integer>>
2. bfsShortestHops(graph, int src) → int[]  (distances from src)
3. countComponents(graph, int n) → int
4. hasCycleUndirected(graph, int n) → boolean
5. isBipartite(graph, int n) → boolean  (BFS 2-colouring)
Include main() tests: disconnected graph, graph with cycle, bipartite grid, star graph.

checklist.text: buildGraph adds both directions for undirected
bfsShortestHops marks visited on enqueue not on poll
countComponents starts fresh DFS for each unvisited node
hasCycleUndirected tracks parent to avoid false positive on the back-edge
isBipartite returns false as soon as any edge connects same-colour nodes

hints: bfsShortestHops: initialise dist[] = -1, set dist[src]=0 before enqueueing src.
hasCycleUndirected: pass parent=-1 for the initial call.
isBipartite: use int[] color = -1/0/1. Start BFS, assign 0 to src, alternate 1-color for neighbours.
